questions.question: Which of the following best describes a computing device?
options: (A) Only a laptop or desktop computer.
(B) Any physical artifact that can run a program.
(C) Only devices that are connected to the Internet.
(D) A program that runs on a computer.
questions.explanation: A computing device is any physical object capable of running a program, including routers, smart sensors, and tablets.

questions.question: What is the difference between bandwidth and latency?
options: (A) Bandwidth is speed, and latency is the amount of data.
(B) Bandwidth is the maximum amount of data that can be sent in a fixed time, while latency is the time it takes for a bit to travel from sender to receiver.
(C) Bandwidth and latency are different words for the same thing.
(D) Latency is measured in bits per second, while bandwidth is measured in milliseconds.
questions.explanation: Bandwidth is the capacity (bits/sec), while latency is the delay (time).

questions.question: Why was IPv6 created?
options: (A) To make the internet faster.
(B) Because the 32-bit addresses of IPv4 were running out.
(C) To replace the DNS system.
(D) To allow for more secure connections.
questions.explanation: IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses to provide a much larger pool of addresses than the 32-bit IPv4.

questions.question: How are IP addresses typically structured?
options: (A) Randomly assigned to every device.
(B) Hierarchically, similar to a physical mailing address.
(C) Alphabetically based on the device owner's name.
(D) Chronologically based on when the device first connected.
questions.explanation: IP addresses are hierarchical (Network -> Subnetwork -> Device) to make routing efficient.

questions.question: What makes the Internet 'fault-tolerant'?
options: (A) It never makes mistakes in data transmission.
(B) It has redundancy, meaning there are multiple paths between devices.
(C) It is owned by a single organization that fixes errors quickly.
(D) It uses only one highly reliable path for all data.
questions.explanation: Redundancy ensures that if one path fails, data can take an alternative route.

questions.question: Which protocol is responsible for breaking data into packets and ensuring they all arrive and are reassembled in the correct order?
options: (A) IP
(B) UDP
(C) TCP
(D) HTTP
questions.explanation: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) provides reliable, ordered delivery of packets.

questions.question: When would a developer choose UDP over TCP?
options: (A) When every single bit of data must be accurate (e.g., file transfer).
(B) When speed is more important than perfect reliability (e.g., live video streaming).
(C) When the data needs to be encrypted.
(D) When the device is not connected to a router.
questions.explanation: UDP is faster because it doesn't check for missing packets, making it ideal for real-time applications.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the Domain Name System (DNS)?
options: (A) To encrypt web pages.
(B) To translate human-readable domain names into IP addresses.
(C) To route packets between networks.
(D) To store the physical files of a website.
questions.explanation: DNS acts as the 'phone book' of the internet, mapping names like google.com to IPs.

questions.question: What is meant by the 'scalability' of the Internet?
options: (A) The ability to measure the speed of the network.
(B) The ability to continue functioning and growing as more devices and networks are added.
(C) The cost of adding new devices to the network.
(D) The physical size of the routers used.
questions.explanation: Scalability is the property of the internet that allows it to grow without needing a fundamental redesign.

questions.question: Why are open standards and protocols (like those from the IETF) important for the Internet?
options: (A) They allow anyone to change the internet's core rules whenever they want.
(B) They ensure that devices from different manufacturers can communicate with each other.
(C) They make the internet free for everyone.
(D) They prevent government regulation of the internet.
questions.explanation: Open protocols ensure interoperability between different hardware and software.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'digital divide'?
options: (A) The difference in speed between download and upload rates.
(B) The gap between those who have access to the Internet and those who do not.
(C) The separation between the hardware and software layers of a network.
(D) The time it takes for a packet to cross the ocean.
questions.explanation: The digital divide refers to the socioeconomic and geographic inequalities in internet access.

questions.question: A message is broken into several packets. Which of the following is true about how these packets travel across the Internet?
options: (A) All packets must follow the exact same path.
(B) Packets may take different paths and arrive out of order.
(C) Packets are sent one by one, and each must be acknowledged before the next is sent.
(D) Packets are only used for small messages; large messages are sent as a single stream.
questions.explanation: Packet switching allows for dynamic routing where packets can take the most efficient path available.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a higher-level protocol using a lower-level protocol?
options: (A) IP using TCP
(B) HTTP using TCP
(C) Ethernet using DNS
(D) TCP using HTTP
questions.explanation: HTTP is an application layer protocol that relies on TCP for reliable transmission.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between the World Wide Web and the Internet?
options: (A) They are exactly the same thing.
(B) The Internet is a network of networks; the World Wide Web is a system of linked pages/files that use the Internet.
(C) The World Wide Web is the hardware, and the Internet is the software.
(D) The Internet is only for email, while the World Wide Web is for browsing.
questions.explanation: The Internet is the infrastructure; the Web is an application that runs on top of it.

questions.question: A network administrator adds several new routers to a network to ensure that if one fails, there is still a connection. This is an example of:
options: (A) Latency
(B) Redundancy
(C) DNS
(D) IP addressing
questions.explanation: Redundancy is the inclusion of extra components so that a system continues to work even if individual components fail.
